title: Maxtor Survey Report (RAMP Results Presentation)
author: Peter S. Kastner; David Hill
abstract: Final 38-slide RAMP results presentation by Peter Kastner and David Hill for Maxtor Corporation, May 2003. Synthesizes findings from 70 telephone survey respondents and 5 face-to-face interviews (Timex, Applied Materials, State Street, Inovant/Visa, Kaiser Permanente) on enterprise willingness to adopt ATA (low-cost/midline) disk as a complementary storage tier. Key findings: 65% of enterprises use 60%+ of available storage; storage growth below vendor projections; 70% have read-only data but cannot easily identify it; 75% likely to purchase low-cost disk; backup/restore remains a major pain point. Key conclusions: MaXLine should be positioned as 'complementary' not 'substitute' storage; 'mid-line' is the correct market term; positioning should emphasize 'better than ATA' rather than 'less than SCSI'; disk-based backup/copy presents a major opportunity.
importance_rationale: Definitive primary-source market validation study for the enterprise midline/nearline storage tier — a market that grew to tens of billions in annual revenue. This presentation directly shaped Maxtor's MaXLine positioning, established 'mid-line' as the industry term, and correctly identified the storage tiering market structure that became the template for all subsequent enterprise storage architecture. Kastner and Hill's findings were the first rigorous enterprise survey data on ATA disk adoption readiness.
relevance_rationale: The core findings remain directly applicable: storage utilization thresholds, the administrative-cost-versus-hardware-cost tension, the backup-to-disk opportunity, and the complementary-versus-substitute positioning debate are all live issues in contemporary storage and cloud markets. The 'midline' positioning framework they defined is still used to describe nearline SAS and capacity-optimized SSD tiers today.
prescience_rationale: This study achieved extraordinary prescience: (1) 'Complementary online storage' — midline/nearline became a dominant enterprise tier (SATA, SAS nearline, capacity HDD); (2) 'Mid-line' term coined here is now standard industry nomenclature; (3) Predicted storage vendor revenue shortfall from slower growth — proved correct 2003-2006; (4) Backup-to-disk opportunity called correctly — disk-based backup/VTL market grew dramatically 2004-2010; (5) ATA → enterprise SATA evolution predicted; (6) ISV partnership requirement for policy-based tiering — proved correct (NetApp ONTAP tiering, HP AutoPath, Symantec LiveState); (7) Complementary positioning beat substitute positioning — Western Digital RE drives struggled while Maxtor/SATA nearline succeeded.
